Xiong Shanjun Review: The Bear Mountain Lord Guide - Empires & Puzzles

Xiong Shanjun is a massive bear with a massive swing in battle momentum. By reducing enemy max health while boosting his own team's HP, he creates a 1600-point gap that's hard to close. But with average speed and aging stats, can this Journey family tank still hold the line? Discover the strategy behind the Mountain Lord.
Xiong Shanjun: The Bear Necessities of Health & Mana Control
Xiong Shanjun, often affectionately (or fearfully) called "The Bear," is a 5-star Dark hero from the
, released during the War of Three Kingdoms era. Originally a somewhat confused hero affecting only "nearby" targets, he received a significant balance update that expanded his chaos to

enemies and allies. He is a attrition-warfare specialist, designed to grind the enemy down by shrinking their health bars while expanding your own, all while manipulating the flow of mana. He's a classic "slow burn" hero who wins not by a single strike, but by making the battle increasingly impossible for the opponent to sustain.

Let's address the bear in the room: Xiong Shanjun is terrifying. This isn't a cuddly teddy bear; this is a magically infused, armored beast screaming into the void. The purple energy crackling from his eyes and the arrows stuck in his armor tell a story of a warrior who simply refuses to go down. The art style is intense, leaning heavily into the "demon beast" aesthetic of the Journey family. It’s a bold look that says, "I will eat your healer."

Extremely low by modern standards, where strikers push 1300+. Thankfully, his main damage source (Health Reduction) is fixed and doesn't rely on this stat.
Respectable for his time, but modern "squishy" heroes now have base defense higher than Xiong's tank-focused stats.

is essentially mandatory just to get his survivability on par with a modern non-limit-broken 5-star.
Special Skill: Fury of the Mountain Lord
Xiong Shanjun's skill is a "four-point swing" that affects the entire board. It’s important to note that this skill was buffed to target
enemies and allies (previously target and nearby), which massively increased his utility.
This is his primary weapon. Unlike standard damage, this lowers the

of the enemy's health. It ignores defense stats and standard damage reduction buffs. Against modern healers who rely on Boosted Health, this is a direct counter, as it shreds that extra HP layer first.
He provides a significant safety net for his team. 200 HP per turn is a solid regeneration rate that can overheal, protecting your team from snipers.

This is arguably his most disruptive trait. Slowing down the entire enemy team by a quarter significantly messes up their timing, often preventing a cleanser from firing before the next tick of damage.
Conversely, he speeds your team up. This dual mana control creates a "tempo gap" where your team fires more often than the enemy.

This is one of the strongest RNG bonuses in the game for a buffer/debuffer.
Every time he casts, there is a coin flip.

Upon release, Xiong Shanjun was criticized for only hitting "nearby" targets, making him an awkward center tank. The subsequent buff to affect "All" enemies redeemed him significantly, turning him into a legitimate war tank option for a period.

have fallen behind. Modern heroes do "more" instantly. For example,
(Dec 2025) boosts health by 1300 instantly. Xiong takes 4 turns to reach 800.
He is too slow and too squishy compared to the current monsters. He will likely die before firing.
In rush wars or standard wars outside the top bracket, his ability to control mana and grind down tanky defenses is still valuable.
Max health reduction is one of the best ways to tackle high-level quest bosses and Towers.

Maximizes his Paladin class talent (chance to boost defense when hit), keeping him alive against snipers.
Low tile damage, but he is not brought for tile damage.

path. Xiong Shanjun contributes zero value if he is dead. His "damage" is fixed (200 per turn), so Attack nodes yield diminishing returns compared to keeping him alive for one more turn of mana suppression.
Simultanously buffs your team's mana and nerfs the enemy's.
His max health reduction effect removes enemy boosted health ("overheal") faster than standard damage.
If the Journey family bonus procs, his skill becomes one of the most damaging/healing single casts in the game (over time).
800 damage is 800 damage, regardless of the enemy's defense stat. This makes him effective against super-high defense tanks.
He offers no immediate "save" or "kill." If your team is at critical health, his HoT might be too slow to save them.
His Max Health Reduction and Mana Debuff are status ailments. A single cleanse (e.g., from Rigard or a modern toon) wipes out half his value immediately.
He is fragile by 2026 standards and requires significant resource investment (Aethers) to survive.
Without a mana troop or rush mode, he often struggles to fire before fast modern threats decimate the team.
At Very Fast speed, he becomes a nightmare. He can fire early, locking the enemy mana down before they can get started.
His max health reduction is permanent (mostly) against enemies and works great against bosses with massive HP pools.

Great for auto-farming hard stages where sustainability is key.
Xiong Shanjun + Queen Guowang + Erlang Shen.
Maximizes the Journey family bonus chance. If Xiong extends his duration, and Queen Guowang extends hers, the team becomes an unstoppable engine of buffs and damage.
Xiong Shanjun + Waterpipe/Lady of the Lake + Proteus/Hel.
Combine Xiong's -24% mana generation with mana reduction minions or mana stops. The enemy will simply never charge.
A heavy purple stack that relies on overheating and outlasting the opponent.
With their high passive resistance to ailments, they can often ignore Xiong's health reduction and mana debuff entirely.
Any hero that cleanses (Ariel, Vivica) neutralizes his offensive pressure.
They reduce damage to 1, effectively ignoring his health reduction tick for the first few hits.

The community's relationship with the Bear has been a rollercoaster.
Upon release, players were vocal about his visual design. One player noted,
"Everytime I see him I feel like he is abused. The mouth just seems very weird."
Many compared him to the classic hero Boss Wolf. One user commented,
"if boss wolf is on steroids. then he will look like this."
Initial reviews were lukewarm due to the "nearby" targeting. After the buff to "All," sentiment shifted to finding him
"Great for me in defence as tank and also useful in offence."

Xiong Shanjun is a hero that demands patience. He doesn't offer the instant gratification of a 600% sniper hit, but his ability to warp the battlefield's math—health and mana—in your favor is undeniable. While his stats have gathered some dust, his kit remains mechanically sound. If you can keep the big bear alive, he will slowly, surely, and painfully grind your enemies into dust.
A solid, utilitarian hero who has transitioned from a main tank to a valuable niche support piece for Rush wars and PvE.
A comprehensive review of Xiong Shanjun, the Journey Family tank. Analyze his max health reduction, mana control, and viability in the modern Empires & Puzzles meta.
